title: Precision and Recall
text: Precision and recall are two metrics for evaluating classification or retrieval systems. Precision is the fraction of items flagged as positive that are actually positive: true positives divided by (true positives plus false positives). Recall is the fraction of actual positives that were correctly flagged: true positives divided by (true positives plus false negatives). The two trade off against each other. A model that flags everything as positive gets perfect recall but terrible precision. A model that only flags items it is very confident about gets high precision but misses many true positives, hurting recall. Which metric matters more depends on the cost of each error type: in spam filtering, false positives (blocking real email) are costly, so precision matters more; in disease screening, false negatives (missing a sick patient) are costly, so recall matters more.

title: F1 Score
text: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, calculated as 2 times precision times recall, divided by the sum of precision and recall. It is used when you want a single number that balances both metrics rather than reporting them separately. The harmonic mean is used instead of a simple average because it penalizes extreme imbalances more heavily: a model with precision of 1.0 and recall of 0.01 gets an F1 score close to 0.02, not 0.5, correctly reflecting that the model is nearly useless despite its perfect precision. F1 is most appropriate when false positives and false negatives are roughly equally costly. When they are not, a weighted variant called F-beta lets you weight recall more or less heavily than precision.

title: Overfitting
text: Overfitting happens when a model learns patterns specific to its training data, including noise and random fluctuations, rather than the underlying relationship that generalizes to new data. An overfit model shows low error on training data but high error on held-out test data. Common causes include a model that is too complex relative to the amount of training data, training for too many iterations, or too many input features relative to the number of examples. Symptoms include a large and growing gap between training and validation loss during training. Common fixes include regularization, early stopping, dropout in neural networks, gathering more training data, or simplifying the model architecture.

title: Underfitting
text: Underfitting happens when a model is too simple to capture the real pattern in the data, resulting in high error on both the training set and the test set. Unlike overfitting, there is no meaningful gap between training and validation performance, because the model performs poorly on both equally. Common causes include using an overly simple model (such as linear regression for a clearly non-linear relationship), too much regularization, or training for too few iterations before the model has had a chance to learn. Fixes include using a more expressive model, adding relevant features, reducing regularization strength, or training for longer.

title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
text: The bias-variance tradeoff describes a fundamental tension in model fitting. Bias is the error introduced by approximating a real-world problem with a simplified model; high-bias models tend to underfit. Variance is the error introduced by a model's sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training data; high-variance models tend to overfit. Total expected prediction error can be decomposed into bias squared, plus variance, plus irreducible noise. Simple models (like linear regression) tend to have high bias and low variance. Complex models (like deep decision trees) tend to have low bias and high variance. The goal of model selection is finding the sweet spot that minimizes the sum of both error sources, not eliminating either one entirely.

title: Cross-Validation
text: Cross-validation is a technique for estimating how well a model will generalize to unseen data, using only the training set. In k-fold cross-validation, the data is split into k roughly equal parts; the model is trained on k-1 parts and evaluated on the remaining part, and this is repeated k times so every part serves as the test set exactly once. The k resulting scores are averaged to produce a more reliable performance estimate than a single train-test split would give, since it reduces the chance that the result is a fluke of one particular split. Common choices are 5-fold or 10-fold. For time series data, standard k-fold is inappropriate because it can leak future information into training; a walk-forward or time-based split should be used instead.

title: Regularization (L1 and L2)
text: Regularization adds a penalty term to a model's loss function to discourage overly complex solutions and reduce overfitting. L2 regularization (ridge) adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared coefficient values, which shrinks all coefficients toward zero smoothly but rarely sets any exactly to zero. L1 regularization (lasso) adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute coefficient values, which can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero, effectively performing feature selection by removing irrelevant variables from the model. Elastic net combines both penalties. The strength of regularization is controlled by a hyperparameter, usually called lambda or alpha, typically chosen via cross-validation.

title: Gradient Descent
text: Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to find the parameter values that minimize a model's loss function. It works by computing the gradient (the direction of steepest increase) of the loss with respect to each parameter, then updating each parameter a small step in the opposite direction. The size of that step is controlled by the learning rate: too large and the algorithm can overshoot the minimum or diverge; too small and training becomes very slow. Variants include stochastic gradient descent, which updates parameters using one training example (or a small mini-batch) at a time instead of the full dataset, making it far more practical for large datasets, and adaptive methods like Adam, which adjust the effective learning rate for each parameter automatically.

title: Logistic Regression
text: Logistic regression is a classification algorithm that models the probability of a binary outcome as a function of input features. Despite the name, it is used for classification, not regression of a continuous value. It works by applying the logistic (sigmoid) function to a linear combination of the input features, squashing the output into the range zero to one, which is interpreted as a probability. The model is typically fit by maximizing the likelihood of the observed labels, which is equivalent to minimizing log loss. Logistic regression is valued for being simple, fast to train, and interpretable: each coefficient represents the change in the log-odds of the outcome per unit increase in that feature.

title: Decision Trees
text: A decision tree is a model that makes predictions by asking a sequence of yes/no questions about input features, splitting the data into smaller and smaller groups until reaching a final prediction at a leaf node. At each split, the algorithm chooses the feature and threshold that best separates the data according to a criterion like Gini impurity or information gain (for classification) or variance reduction (for regression). Decision trees are easy to interpret and visualize, and require little data preprocessing, but a single deep tree tends to overfit badly because it can keep splitting until every leaf contains just one training example. Limiting tree depth, requiring a minimum number of samples per leaf, or pruning after training are common ways to control this.

title: Random Forests
text: A random forest is an ensemble method that combines many decision trees to produce a more accurate and stable prediction than any single tree. Each tree is trained on a random bootstrap sample of the training data, and at each split, only a random subset of features is considered, which decorrelates the trees from one another. For classification, the forest's prediction is the majority vote across all trees; for regression, it is the average. Because individual trees tend to overfit but in different, somewhat random ways, averaging many of them cancels out much of that overfitting, substantially reducing variance compared to a single deep tree while keeping bias roughly the same.

title: Gradient Boosting
text: Gradient boosting is an ensemble technique that builds models sequentially, where each new model is trained to correct the errors made by the combination of all previous models. Unlike random forests, which build many independent trees and average them, boosting builds trees one at a time, each one fitting the residual errors of the current ensemble, weighted by a learning rate that controls how much each new tree contributes. This sequential, error-correcting approach often achieves higher accuracy than random forests on structured, tabular data, but is more prone to overfitting if too many trees are added or the learning rate is too high, and is generally slower to train since trees cannot be built in parallel. Popular implementations include XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost.

title: Propensity Score Matching
text: Propensity score matching is a causal inference technique used to estimate a treatment effect from observational (non-randomized) data. It works by first estimating each unit's propensity score, the probability of receiving treatment given observed characteristics, typically with a logistic regression model. Treated units are then matched to untreated units with similar propensity scores, on the logic that two units with the same score looked equally likely to receive treatment, so comparing their outcomes approximates a randomized comparison. This only works if all relevant confounding variables were observed and included in the propensity model; any unmeasured confounder will still bias the result. It is most useful when there is good overlap, or common support, between the treated and untreated groups' propensity score distributions.

title: Inverse Probability Weighting
text: Inverse probability weighting (IPW) is a causal inference technique that corrects for confounding by reweighting observations rather than discarding any of them, which is what matching often does. Each unit is weighted by the inverse of its probability of receiving the treatment it actually received, estimated via a propensity score model. This means a control-group unit that looked very likely to be treated, based on its characteristics, gets a large weight, since it is rare and informative, while a typical control unit gets a smaller weight. After reweighting, the comparison group's covariate distribution should resemble the treated group's, removing the confounding bias under the assumption that all relevant confounders were measured. A known weakness is that very small propensity scores in the denominator can produce extremely large, unstable weights.

title: Confounding Variables
text: A confounding variable is a factor that influences both the treatment (or exposure) being studied and the outcome being measured, creating a spurious association between the two even if no true causal relationship exists. For example, in observational data, people who exercise more might also eat healthier diets; if diet is not accounted for, exercise's relationship with health outcomes could be partly or entirely attributable to diet instead. Confounding is the central problem that distinguishes correlation from causation, and is the reason randomized experiments are considered the gold standard for causal claims: random assignment ensures that, on average, treatment and control groups are balanced on all confounders, measured or not. Techniques like propensity score matching, inverse probability weighting, and regression adjustment attempt to correct for confounding in non-randomized data, but only for confounders that were actually measured.

title: A/B Testing
text: A/B testing is a randomized experiment used to compare two or more versions of something, commonly a website, app feature, or marketing message, by randomly assigning users to each version and comparing an outcome metric, such as click-through rate or conversion rate. Random assignment is what makes A/B tests a reliable way to establish causation rather than mere correlation, since it balances both observed and unobserved characteristics between groups on average. Key design considerations include determining an adequate sample size in advance using a power analysis, avoiding 'peeking' at results repeatedly before the planned sample size is reached (which inflates the false positive rate), and choosing a primary metric in advance rather than searching after the fact for any metric that happened to show a significant difference.

title: P-values and Statistical Significance
text: A p-value is the probability of observing a result at least as extreme as the one obtained, assuming the null hypothesis (typically, that there is no real effect) is true. A small p-value suggests the observed data would be unlikely if there were truly no effect, which is taken as evidence against the null hypothesis. A common but somewhat arbitrary threshold is 0.05: if the p-value falls below it, the result is called 'statistically significant.' A p-value is not the probability that the null hypothesis is true, and a statistically significant result is not automatically practically important; with a large enough sample size, even a tiny, meaningless effect can produce a very small p-value. Conversely, a true effect can fail to reach significance simply because the sample size was too small.

title: Type I and Type II Errors
text: A Type I error occurs when a statistical test incorrectly rejects a true null hypothesis, concluding there is an effect when there actually is not; this is also called a false positive. A Type II error occurs when a test fails to reject a false null hypothesis, concluding there is no effect when one actually exists; this is a false negative. The significance level, usually denoted alpha and commonly set to 0.05, is the rate of Type I errors a test is designed to tolerate. The Type II error rate is denoted beta, and statistical power, equal to 1 minus beta, is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect when one exists. Increasing sample size generally increases power, reducing the Type II error rate, without changing the Type I error rate.

title: Central Limit Theorem
text: The central limit theorem states that the distribution of the sample mean of a sufficiently large number of independent, identically distributed random variables approaches a normal distribution, regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, as long as that distribution has finite variance. This is why normal-distribution-based methods, such as confidence intervals and many hypothesis tests, can be applied to sample means even when the underlying data is skewed or not normally distributed at all, provided the sample size is large enough. In practice, a sample size of around 30 is often cited as a rough rule of thumb for the approximation to be reasonable, though more heavily skewed underlying distributions require larger samples before the approximation holds well.

title: Standard Deviation vs Standard Error
text: Standard deviation measures how spread out individual values are around the mean of a single sample or population. Standard error measures how much the sample mean itself would be expected to vary if you repeated the sampling process many times; it is calculated as the standard deviation divided by the square root of the sample size. As sample size increases, standard deviation of the underlying data stays roughly the same, but standard error shrinks, reflecting the fact that the mean of a larger sample is a more precise estimate of the true population mean. Confidence intervals and statistical tests on means are built from the standard error, not the raw standard deviation, which is a common point of confusion.

title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
text: Principal component analysis is a dimensionality reduction technique that transforms a set of correlated features into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered so that the first component captures the most variance in the data, the second captures the next most variance subject to being uncorrelated with the first, and so on. PCA is computed by finding the eigenvectors of the data's covariance matrix. It is commonly used to reduce the number of features before modeling, to visualize high-dimensional data in two or three dimensions, or to remove multicollinearity among input features. A downside is that the resulting components are linear combinations of the original features and often lose direct interpretability, since a component is rarely a single, clearly meaningful real-world quantity.

title: K-Means Clustering
text: K-means is an unsupervised clustering algorithm that partitions data into k groups by iteratively assigning each point to its nearest cluster center, then recalculating each cluster center as the mean of the points assigned to it, repeating until the assignments stop changing. The number of clusters, k, must be chosen in advance; common methods for choosing it include the elbow method, which plots within-cluster variance against k and looks for a bend, or the silhouette score, which measures how well-separated clusters are. K-means assumes clusters are roughly spherical and similarly sized, and can perform poorly on clusters with irregular shapes or very different sizes. Because it starts from randomly chosen initial cluster centers, results can vary between runs, so it is common practice to run it multiple times and keep the best result.

title: Neural Networks Basics
text: A neural network is a model composed of layers of interconnected nodes, called neurons, where each connection has an associated weight. Each neuron computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias term, and passes the result through a non-linear activation function, such as ReLU or sigmoid, before passing it to the next layer. Without a non-linear activation function, stacking layers would be mathematically equivalent to a single linear transformation, no matter how many layers were added, which is why non-linearity is essential for a network to learn complex patterns. A network with at least one hidden layer between the input and output layers is generally called a deep neural network once it has multiple hidden layers. The weights are learned by minimizing a loss function via gradient descent and backpropagation.

title: Backpropagation
text: Backpropagation is the algorithm used to efficiently compute the gradient of a neural network's loss function with respect to every weight in the network, which is what gradient descent needs in order to update those weights. It works by applying the chain rule from calculus, propagating the error backward from the output layer to the input layer, layer by layer, reusing intermediate computations rather than recalculating each gradient from scratch. This makes training networks with millions of parameters computationally feasible. A known difficulty is the vanishing gradient problem, where gradients become extremely small as they are propagated back through many layers, making early layers learn very slowly; this motivated activation functions like ReLU and architectural innovations like residual connections, both of which help gradients flow more effectively through deep networks.

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text: A convolutional neural network (CNN) is a neural network architecture designed primarily for image and grid-like data. Instead of connecting every input to every neuron, as a standard fully connected layer does, a convolutional layer applies a small set of learnable filters that slide across the input, each detecting a specific local pattern, such as an edge or texture, regardless of where it appears in the image. This gives CNNs two useful properties: far fewer parameters than a fully connected layer of comparable size, and translation invariance, meaning a feature is detected the same way no matter where it appears. Pooling layers are often used between convolutional layers to reduce the spatial size of the data and add some robustness to small shifts or distortions in the input.

title: Recurrent Neural Networks and LSTM
text: A recurrent neural network (RNN) is designed for sequential data, such as text or time series, by maintaining a hidden state that is updated at each time step and carries information forward from previous steps in the sequence. Plain RNNs struggle to learn long-range dependencies because gradients tend to vanish or explode as they are propagated back through many time steps. Long short-term memory networks (LSTMs) address this with a more complex internal structure involving gates that explicitly control what information is added to, removed from, or read from the cell's memory at each step, allowing the network to retain relevant information over much longer sequences. LSTMs were the dominant architecture for sequence modeling before being largely superseded by transformer-based models for most large-scale applications.

title: Transformers and the Attention Mechanism
text: The transformer is a neural network architecture built around the attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the relevance of every other token in a sequence when processing a given token, rather than processing tokens strictly in order as recurrent networks do. Self-attention computes, for each token, a weighted combination of all other tokens' representations, with weights determined by how relevant each pair of tokens is to each other, learned during training. Because this computation can be parallelized across the whole sequence at once, transformers train much faster on modern hardware than recurrent architectures of comparable size, and they capture long-range dependencies more directly. Transformers are the architecture behind most modern large language models, including BERT and GPT.

title: Word Embeddings
text: Word embeddings are dense vector representations of words, designed so that words with similar meanings or usage patterns end up close together in the resulting vector space, and relationships between words are reflected in geometric relationships between vectors. Early embedding methods like Word2Vec and GloVe assign one fixed vector per word, learned from how often words co-occur in large amounts of text, so the same word always has the same vector regardless of context. This is a limitation for words with multiple meanings. Contextual embeddings, produced by models like BERT, instead generate a different vector for a word depending on the surrounding sentence, allowing the same word to be represented differently depending on which meaning is intended in that specific context.

title: BERT
text: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is a transformer-based language model designed to produce rich contextual representations of text, pretrained on a large amount of unlabeled text using two objectives: predicting randomly masked words within a sentence using context from both directions, and predicting whether two sentences originally followed each other in the source text. The 'bidirectional' part is key: unlike earlier left-to-right language models, BERT's masked-word prediction task lets it use context from both before and after a given word. After pretraining, BERT is typically fine-tuned on a smaller, labeled dataset for a specific downstream task, such as sentiment classification or question answering, which made it a very effective general-purpose starting point rather than training a task-specific model entirely from scratch.

title: GPT and Autoregressive Language Models
text: GPT (Generative Pretrained Transformer) is a family of autoregressive language models, meaning they generate text one token at a time, with each new token predicted based on all the tokens that came before it in the sequence. Unlike BERT's bidirectional masked-word objective, GPT models are trained purely left-to-right: predict the next word, given everything before it, repeated across enormous amounts of text. This training objective alone, at sufficient scale, gives rise to a wide range of capabilities beyond simple text completion, including translation, summarization, and question answering, often without any task-specific fine-tuning at all, a property generally called few-shot or zero-shot learning. Text is generated by repeatedly sampling or selecting the next most likely token and feeding it back in as part of the input for predicting the following token.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
text: Retrieval-augmented generation is an approach for answering questions or generating text using a large language model combined with an external knowledge source, rather than relying solely on what the model memorized during training. Given a query, a retriever first searches a document collection for the passages most relevant to that query, then those passages are inserted into the language model's prompt as context, and the model generates its answer grounded in that retrieved text. This approach reduces hallucination, since the model has actual source material to draw from rather than generating purely from its trained parameters, and it allows the system's knowledge to be updated simply by changing the underlying document collection, without retraining the model itself. The quality of a RAG system depends heavily on the quality of the retriever, since the language model can only be as accurate as the context it is given.

title: BM25
text: BM25 is a ranking function used in information retrieval to score how relevant a document is to a search query, based on the frequency of query terms appearing in the document. It improves on simpler term-frequency scoring in two key ways: term frequency saturates, meaning a term appearing 10 times contributes less additional score than the jump from 0 to 1 occurrence would, since after a point additional repetitions provide diminishing evidence of relevance; and it normalizes for document length, since longer documents naturally contain more word occurrences without necessarily being more relevant. BM25 also weights rarer terms across the whole collection more heavily than common terms, similar in spirit to the idea behind TF-IDF. Despite being decades old, it remains a very strong, fast baseline for keyword-based search, often competitive with much more complex neural retrieval methods, especially on short queries with clear keyword overlap.

title: TF-IDF
text: TF-IDF (term frequency-inverse document frequency) is a numerical statistic used to measure how important a word is to a specific document within a larger collection of documents. Term frequency measures how often a word appears in a given document. Inverse document frequency down-weights words that appear in many documents across the collection, on the logic that a word appearing in almost every document carries little information about what makes any particular document distinctive; conversely, a word that appears in few documents but appears often in one of them is a strong signal that document is specifically about that topic. A document can be represented as a vector of TF-IDF scores, one per vocabulary word, and documents can then be compared using cosine similarity between their vectors. TF-IDF is a precursor to and conceptually related to BM25, though BM25 generally performs better in practice for ranking search results.

title: Cosine Similarity
text: Cosine similarity measures how similar two vectors are by computing the cosine of the angle between them, calculated as their dot product divided by the product of their magnitudes. The result ranges from negative one (pointing in opposite directions) to one (pointing in exactly the same direction), with zero meaning the vectors are orthogonal, or unrelated, in the represented space. Unlike Euclidean distance, cosine similarity ignores the magnitude of the vectors entirely and only considers their direction, which makes it well suited for comparing text represented as TF-IDF or embedding vectors, since a long document and a short document discussing the same topic should be considered similar even though the long document's vector has a much larger magnitude.

title: ROC Curve and AUC
text: A ROC (receiver operating characteristic) curve plots a binary classifier's true positive rate against its false positive rate as the decision threshold for classifying something as positive is varied from very strict to very lenient. AUC, the area under that curve, summarizes the curve in a single number ranging from 0.5 (no better than random guessing) to 1.0 (perfect separation between classes). Unlike accuracy, AUC does not depend on choosing a single decision threshold in advance, and it is relatively robust to class imbalance, since it considers the full range of possible thresholds rather than performance at one fixed cutoff. A common misconception is that AUC alone tells you whether a specific threshold is good for a specific business use case; it summarizes overall ranking quality across all thresholds, not performance at the particular threshold you'll actually deploy.

title: Confusion Matrix
text: A confusion matrix is a table summarizing the performance of a classification model by cross-tabulating predicted labels against actual labels. For a binary classifier, it has four cells: true positives (correctly predicted positive), true negatives (correctly predicted negative), false positives (incorrectly predicted positive, also called a Type I error), and false negatives (incorrectly predicted negative, also called a Type II error). Precision, recall, F1 score, and accuracy can all be derived directly from the four values in this table. For problems with more than two classes, the confusion matrix expands to an N-by-N grid, with the diagonal representing correct predictions for each class and off-diagonal cells showing which classes are most often confused with one another.

title: Imbalanced Datasets and SMOTE
text: An imbalanced dataset is one where the classes being predicted are not represented in roughly equal proportions, such as fraud detection, where fraudulent transactions might make up far less than one percent of all transactions. Standard accuracy is a misleading metric on imbalanced data, since a model that simply predicts the majority class every time can achieve very high accuracy while being completely useless for the actual task. Common strategies include using metrics other than accuracy, such as precision, recall, F1, or AUC; adjusting the classification threshold; and resampling the training data, either by undersampling the majority class or oversampling the minority class. SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) oversamples the minority class by generating new synthetic examples along the line segments connecting existing minority-class examples to their nearest neighbors, rather than simply duplicating existing examples, which would otherwise risk causing the model to overfit to a small set of repeated points.

title: Time Series Forecasting and ARIMA
text: Time series forecasting predicts future values of a sequence based on its past values, where, unlike most other machine learning problems, the order of observations matters and cannot be shuffled. ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) is a classical statistical forecasting model with three components: the autoregressive part predicts a value using a linear combination of its own past values; the integrated part applies differencing, subtracting each value from the previous one, to make a non-stationary series (one whose statistical properties change over time) stationary; and the moving average part models the relationship between an observation and the past forecast errors. A critical practice in evaluating any time series model is using walk-forward validation rather than a standard random train-test split, since randomly shuffling time-ordered data would let the model train on future information not actually available at prediction time.
